title: Man City vs Tottenham - CL Quarter-Final
summary: Raheem Sterling scored what appeared to be a winner in injury time to send City through on away goals. VAR reviewed the goal and disallowed it after a lengthy check, finding Sergio Aguero's arm was in an offside position. Tottenham advanced instead.
situation_prefill: Sergio Aguero plays the ball in the build-up to a goal. His raised arm extends beyond the line of the last defender. The goal is scored by a teammate. VAR reviews whether the arm counts as an offside body part under Law 11.
why_infamous: Decided who went to the semi-finals. The arm-offside rule was almost unknown to fans before this moment. Review took over 4 minutes.

title: France vs Tunisia - World Cup Group Stage
summary: Antoine Griezmann scored a dramatic injury-time equalizer. The goal was allowed and players celebrated, then VAR intervened to disallow it for offside after the final whistle had already blown. The stadium was left in chaos.
situation_prefill: Antoine Griezmann receives the ball in the penalty area and scores. A French attacker is positioned ahead of the last Tunisian defender when the pass is played. VAR reviews the offside line and the timing of the pass.
why_infamous: Goal was allowed, players celebrated, then disallowed after the whistle. Chaos followed. Tunisia had already been eliminated but the sequence was unprecedented.

title: West Ham vs Aston Villa - Premier League
teams: West Ham United vs Aston Villa
summary: Tomas Soucek's arm blocked a cross in the penalty area. VAR reviewed the incident and awarded a penalty to Aston Villa under the new handball rules, which penalise arms that are in an unnatural position even when the player does not intend to handle the ball.
situation_prefill: A cross is played into the penalty area. West Ham's Tomas Soucek is in the box with his arm extended slightly away from his body. The ball strikes his arm. He does not appear to intentionally control the ball. VAR reviews whether this is handball under Law 12.
why_infamous: Sparked widespread debate about intent vs position in handball decisions. The unnatural arm position rule continues to confuse players and fans.

title: Bournemouth vs Wolves - FA Cup 5th Round
summary: VAR reviewed an offside call and the semi-automated offside technology (SAOT) failed, causing a delay of over 7 minutes. The system could not complete the automated limb-mapping. The review had to be completed manually, and the goal was eventually disallowed.
situation_prefill: A Bournemouth attacker scores a goal. An attacking player is potentially in an offside position when the ball was played. VAR initiates a semi-automated offside review. The goal is under review for whether any part of the attacker's body that can score a goal is ahead of the last defender.
why_infamous: The longest-ever VAR review in English football at that point. SAOT technology publicly failed in real time. 7+ minutes of players standing around waiting.

title: Brazil vs Switzerland - World Cup Group Stage
summary: Casemiro's header appeared to be blocked by Manuel Akanji's hand inside the penalty area. VAR reviewed the incident for a potential handball penalty. After a lengthy review, no penalty was awarded because Akanji's arm was judged to be in a natural position against his body.
situation_prefill: A header from Casemiro is blocked near the goal line by Switzerland defender Manuel Akanji. The ball strikes Akanji's arm. VAR reviews whether this is a handball offence under Law 12, specifically whether the arm was in an unnatural position.
why_infamous: Showed the fine margin between handball and no handball. Akanji's arm was close to his body but the ball still hit it. Highlighted how subjective 'natural position' really is.
